role_summary: Tougaloo College student and CORE activist in Jackson, Mississippi; one of the four sit-in participants at the May 28, 1963 Jackson Woolworth's lunch counter (alongside Joan Trumpauer Mulholland, catalog entry 60, John Salter, and Memphis Norman) whose photographs became among the most-circulated of the Mississippi movement; author of the 1968 memoir Coming of Age in Mississippi (The Dial Press), the canonical first-person account of growing up Black in segregated Mississippi and entering the civil rights movement.
ai_reading: Moody is named on two other catalog pages, most directly the Joan Trumpauer Mulholland (entry 60) page that documents the May 28, 1963 Woolworth's sit-in the two participated in alongside John Salter and Memphis Norman, and a gallery caption on the Frankye Adams-Johnson (entry 43) page that marks the same sit-in. The three verbatim passages on this page are all drawn from Mulholland's oral history, where she places Moody beside her at the Woolworth's counter and on the return trip from the March on Washington. She is not herself a Civil Rights History Project interviewee (she had largely withdrawn from public life by the time the project began in 2010 and died in 2015). Her standalone catalog page is added because Coming of Age in Mississippi is the canonical first-person Mississippi-movement memoir, the kind of textual anchor the catalog's Mississippi-organizing cohort (McLaurin entry 17, Connor entry 96, Adams-Johnson entry 43, Burns entry 70, Mulholland entry 60) implicitly references when describing the texture of their own organizing arc. The novel observation her page surfaces is the memoir-as-organizing-document signature: Moody's 1968 book reached a wide white college audience in the late 1960s, recruiting a generation of organizers; the catalog's Northern-volunteer voices (the Bassetts at entry 38, the Ackermans at 135, Steven McNichols at 118) testify to the way movement-narrative texts produced this kind of cross-region recruitment, with Coming of Age in Mississippi as the dominant single text in that channel.
biographical_paragraph: Moody was born Essie Mae Moody on September 15, 1940 in Wilkinson County, Mississippi, the daughter of Fred Moody and Elmire Williams; she renamed herself Anne in her teens. She grew up in poverty in rural Mississippi and attended Natchez Junior College on a basketball scholarship before transferring to Tougaloo College (the historically Black college outside Jackson) on an academic scholarship. At Tougaloo she joined the NAACP and the Congress of Racial Equality. On May 28, 1963 she participated in the sit-in at the F.W. Woolworth's lunch counter on Capitol Street in downtown Jackson alongside Tougaloo professor John Salter Jr., Joan Trumpauer Mulholland (catalog entry 60), and Memphis Norman; the photographs of white observers pouring ketchup, mustard, and sugar on the seated protesters circulated in Life magazine and across the international press. She worked with CORE in Canton, Mississippi during 1963-1964 organizing voter registration drives. Her 1968 memoir Coming of Age in Mississippi (The Dial Press) recounted her childhood, her time at Tougaloo, and her civil rights organizing; the book became a foundational text of the movement, widely assigned in college courses for the next half-century. She moved to New York in the late 1960s, married Austin Straus in 1967 (later divorced), and largely withdrew from public life from the 1970s onward. She returned to Gloster, Mississippi in her later years and died there on February 5, 2015 at seventy-four.

interview_snippets.speaker: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
interview_snippets.lead_in: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland, who sat at the Jackson Woolworth's counter beside Moody on May 28, 1963, describes the two of them and professor John R. Salter Jr. as the mob poured food on them:
interview_snippets.quote: And there's Annie looking like a skunk with the white sugar on her head and me with all sorts of nice condiments and John R. Salter Jr,

interview_snippets.speaker: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
interview_snippets.lead_in: Mulholland recounts being pulled off her stool and getting back to the counter to sit with Moody, pairing a Black and a white student at each spot:
interview_snippets.quote: But we were able to get loose and get back to the counter, but not back to where Perleana was. And Lois sat with Perleana, So you had a black and white girl here, and Annie and I sat black and white girl here.

interview_snippets.speaker: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked about returning from the August 1963 March on Washington, Mulholland names her travel companions, identifying Annie as the author of Coming of Age in Mississippi:
interview_snippets.quote: Yeah, Ed and Annie and I came back with the King.

sources.title: Anne Moody, Coming of Age in Mississippi
sources.publisher: The Dial Press, 1968 (reprinted Delta, 2004); WorldCat OCLC 445027

sources.title: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (catalog entry 60; the source interview for the three about-Moody passages on this page)
